original_abstract: Abstract. Wood-burning aerosol produced under smoldering conditions was photochemically aged with different relative humidity (RH) and NOx conditions using a 104 m3 dual outdoor chamber under natural sunlight. Light absorption of organic carbon (OC) was measured over the course of photooxidation using a UV–visible spectrometer connected to an integrating sphere. At high RH, the color decayed rapidly. NOx slightly prolonged the color of wood smoke, suggesting that NOx promotes the formation of chromophores via secondary processes. Overall, the mass absorption cross section (integrated between 280 and 600 nm) of OC increased by 11–54% (except high RH) in the morning and then gradually decreased by 19–68% in the afternoon. This dynamic change in light absorption of wood-burning OC can be explained by two mechanisms: chromophore formation and sunlight bleaching. To investigate the effect of chemical transformation on light absorption, wood smoke particles were characterized using various spectrometers. The intensity of fluorescence, which is mainly related to polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs), rapidly decreased with time, indicating the potential bleaching of PAHs. A decline of levoglucosan concentrations evinced the change of primary organic aerosol with time. The aerosol water content measured by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy showed that wood-burning aerosol became less hygroscopic as photooxidation proceeded. A similar trend in light absorption changes has been observed in ambient smoke aerosol originating from the 2012 County Line wildfire in Florida. We conclude that the biomass-burning OC becomes less light absorbing after 8–9 h sunlight exposure compared to fresh wood-burning OC.
humanized_abstract: Wood smoke aerosol emitted during smoldering combustion was photochemically aged in a 104 m3 dual chamber system operated under varied relative humidity (RH) and NOx conditions. The mass light absorption of organic carbon (OC) aerosol was monitored using an integrating sphere-UV-visible spectrometer before and during photochemical aging in direct sun. It was observed that the color of wood smoke was decayed faster at higher RH, whereas its retention was slightly higher at higher NOx levels. This also suggests that NOx species affect chromophore formation through secondary processes. The mass absorption cross section of OC aerosol in the UV-Visible range (280-600 nm) was observed to be elevated by 11–54% during morning hours (after photochemical aging of 2–3 hr) and then started to fall-off to the baseline values by 19–68% during afternoon hours (after photochemical aging of 8–10 hr). Possible causes for this increase and fall-off in light absorption with time, due to the photchemical oxidation of wood smoke aerosol precursors may be attributed to increase in formation of chromophores and sunlight-induced bleaching. To confirm this a detailed study of composition of aged and fresh wood smoke aerosol precursors was carried out. PAH fluorescence intensities showed that fresh PAHs decreased with photochemical aging time, indicating that sunlight had bleached the fluorescent PAH species in the smoke aerosol precursors. Similarly, the concentration of levoglucosan a carbohydrate-like sugar used as an indicator of primary OA precursors, significantly decreased after photochemical aging suggesting chemical modification of smoke aerosol precursors. Aerosol water content studies using FTIR spectrometry suggested hygroscopicity decrease with photochemical aging of the smoke. Ambient OC aerosol was sampled 50 km off-shore of County Line wildfire in Northern Florida 2012 and post photochemical exposures exhibited similar pattern of changes as observed in chamber studies, thus confirmed that the biomass aerosol emissions has the potential of becoming less light absorbing after prolonged photochemical exposure i.e., after about 8-9 hr photochemical aging in direct sunlight.
